1: The theme of divine election operates here. Kish is “a man of great standing” (gibbôr ḥayil) — a qualifier for social standing and military valor. The Benjaminite genealogy grounds Saul in the smallest tribe of Israel, a detail the narrative will emphasize (v21). The trajectory from small tribe to anointed king prefigures the pattern of divine election: God consistently chooses the smaller, the less impressive, the overlooked. The ultimate expression of this pattern is the Incarnation — the King of kings born in an obscure province, in a manger, of a lineage that ran through Bethlehem rather than Jerusalem’s court.
2: A shadow: Saul’s physical perfection — “no one among the Israelites was more handsome; from his shoulders up he stood taller than all the people” — establishes the external standard for kingship that will be explicitly rejected when David is chosen (1 Samuel 16:7: “man looks at the outward appearance, but the LORD looks at the heart”). Saul embodies the kind of king the people desired — impressive, tall, conventionally kingly. The Servant of Isaiah 53:2 has “no beauty or majesty to attract us to him, nothing in his appearance that we should desire him.” The true Anointed One inverts Saul’s standard entirely. Height above others becomes descent below all — the King who stoops to wash feet (John 13:5).
3: The theme of providential searching runs through the biblical narrative. Saul goes looking for lost donkeys and finds a kingdom. The same providential pattern is explicit in Jacob’s sons going to Egypt for grain and finding Joseph; in Ruth going to glean in Boaz’s field. The great reversal — the seeker becomes the found — is the structure of grace. Christ’s parables of the lost sheep, the lost coin, and the lost son (Luke 15) all enact this reversal: what was lost is found by the one who seeks. The Father “goes out” to meet the returning prodigal; YHWH sends Samuel to intercept Saul on his way to nowhere important.
4: The theme of persistence through fruitless searching. Saul traverses multiple regions without finding the donkeys — Ephraim, Shalisha, Shalim, Benjamin. The futility of the search before divine appointment is thematically parallel to human searching apart from God: effort without arrival. The disciples fishing all night and catching nothing (John 21:3) follows the same pattern — exhausted, fruitless labor followed by the encounter with Christ that transforms everything. Providence operates not in spite of the fruitless search but through it.
5: Saul’s practical reasoning — “let’s go back, or my father will stop worrying about the donkeys and start worrying about us” — is interrupted by the servant’s suggestion about the man of God. The divine appointment arrives at the moment of turning back. Canonically, this is the structure of encounter with the prophetic word: when human initiative is exhausted, the divine word arrives. Christ’s encounters follow this pattern — the disciples are ready to give up on the night’s fishing (John 21:3), the Emmaus disciples have given up on their hope (“we had hoped that he was the one”, Luke 24:21) at the very moment Christ reveals himself.
6: The servant’s description of Samuel — “a man of God, a man of distinction whose every word proves true” (ʾîš ʾelōhîm nibbād... kol ʾăšer yəḏabbēr bōʾ yāḇōʾ) — anticipates the criterion for true prophecy in Deuteronomy 18:22 and points forward to the Prophet greater than Samuel (Deuteronomy 18:15). Samuel’s every word coming true is the attribute that the NT applies to Christ’s word: “Heaven and earth will pass away, but my words will never pass away” (Matthew 24:35). The reliable prophetic word that never fails is fulfilled absolutely only in Christ, the Word incarnate (John 1:14).
7: The question of what gift to bring the man of God — and the servant’s quarter-shekel as the only available offering — is a shadow of the empty-handed coming to Christ that the gospel requires. Saul and his servant have nothing worthy to offer; yet they are received, fed at the head of the table, and given the kingdom. The grace-structure here anticipates the gospel pattern: those who come to Christ with nothing are given everything. The parable of the wedding banquet (Matt 22) precisely inverts the gift-logic: the guests are invited without bringing gifts; the feast is provided entirely by the host.
8: The servant’s quarter-shekel is a small thing offered in faith — all that was available, given without hesitation. The theme of small offering made in faith that opens divine provision runs through Scripture: the widow’s two mites (Mark 12:42-44), the boy’s five loaves and two fish (John 6:9), the jar of oil that multiplies (2 Kings 4:2-6). The NT principle that giving what you have in faith — even when it is little — unlocks divine multiplication is foreshadowed here.
9: The editorial note about the transition from “seer” (rōʾeh) to “prophet” (nābîʾ) marks a shift in prophetic office that points toward the one Prophet who fulfills both roles — and more. The seer sees what is hidden; the prophet speaks what YHWH commands. Christ is the one in whom all prophetic vision and speech are unified: he sees the Father (John 6:46), speaks the Father’s words (John 12:49-50), and is himself the Word (John 1:1). The entire succession of prophetic office — from seer to prophet to Christ — is a preparation for the one in whom all prophecy finds its voice.
10: The theme of going to meet the one who has been prepared — Saul goes to the city not knowing Samuel has already been told he is coming. YHWH had prepared both the encounter and the messenger before Saul arrived. The pre-arranged meeting is a shadow of how the divine initiative always precedes the human response: “We love because he first loved us” (1 John 4:19). In Christ, the divine preparation is absolute — chosen “before the creation of the world” (Ephesians 1:4) to meet humanity at its point of need.
11: The young women drawing water who direct Saul to Samuel — unnamed, incidental, used providentially to guide Saul to his destiny. The theme of providential guidance through ordinary persons and encounters runs through the Gospels: the Samaritan woman at the well (John 4) points others to Jesus; the disciples are directed to a man carrying a water jar (Luke 22:10). Providence works through the unremarkable and the ordinary to accomplish its purposes.
12: Samuel has come for the sacrifice at the high place, and the meal is being prepared in anticipation of his arrival. The sequence — the sacrifice, the meal, the honoured guest — prefigures the structure of covenant fellowship: sacrifice first (atonement), then meal (communion). This is the pattern of the Passover (sacrifice and meal), the peace offering, and ultimately the Lord’s Supper — instituted at a meal after the sacrifice of Christ is accomplished.
13: Samuel’s blessing before the feast — “he pronounces the blessing over the sacrifice; only then do the guests eat” — positions Samuel as the one whose blessing sanctifies the meal. Christ is the one whose blessing transforms the common meal into covenant communion: at the Last Supper he took bread, “and when he had given thanks, he broke it” (1 Corinthians 11:24). The presiding prophet-priest at the meal who blesses before others eat is the pattern that Christ fulfills in person.
14: The providential meeting at the gate — the two going in opposite directions who nevertheless arrive at the same moment. Samuel was “coming out toward them” just as they entered. Divine orchestration of encounters at city gates recurs in the OT: Boaz meets Ruth’s kinsman-redeemer at the gate (Ruth 4:1); the man who finds Joseph is “wandering in the fields” when Joseph has lost his way (Genesis 37:15). The Christ who meets us going in the opposite direction — coming toward us while we are heading away — is the pattern of grace that this providential encounter embodies.
15: YHWH’s advance disclosure to Samuel — “the day before Saul arrived” — establishes the pattern of divine foreknowledge preceding election. YHWH did not react to Saul’s arrival; he had already told Samuel what was coming. The revelation before the event is the structure of prophetic anticipation: God announces what he will do before he does it (Amos 3:7: “the Sovereign LORD does nothing without revealing his plan to his servants the prophets”). The ultimate pre-announcement is Christ: foretold throughout the OT before appearing in history. The divine disclosure in advance is a revelation of God as the one who acts sovereignly and then reveals his action rather than reacting to human events.
16: A direct anticipation: the formula in v16 — “I have seen the suffering of my people and their cry has come up to me” — directly echoes the Exodus calling (Exodus 3:7-9: “I have indeed seen the misery of my people... I have heard them crying out... I am concerned about their suffering”). The anointing of Saul is framed as a new Exodus: the same divine compassion that sent Moses now sends an anointed king. But the pattern awaits its complete fulfillment in Christ, whose coming is precisely YHWH hearing the cry of a humanity enslaved not merely to political oppressors but to sin and death. The cry of the world that climaxes in the Incarnation is the ultimate fulfillment of the “cry has come up to me” formula.
17: YHWH points out Saul to Samuel: “Here is the man I told you about — he will govern my people.” The divine identification of the elect king is the moment of divine designation. The same pattern occurs at Christ’s baptism when the Father identifies him: “This is my Son, whom I love; with him I am well pleased” (Matthew 3:17). The divine finger pointing at the elect king — YHWH identifying his chosen to his prophetic representative — is the inauguration of messianic office. The verb yaʿăṣōr (will govern/restrain) is a limited commission; the one who truly governs without limit is the one of whom Isaiah 9:7 says “of the greatness of his government and peace there will be no end.”
18: Saul asks Samuel where to find the seer — not knowing he is already speaking with the very person he seeks. The irony of the unconscious encounter with the one you were looking for is a shadow of humanity’s encounter with Christ: those who asked “where can we find God?” were already standing in front of him (John 14:9: “Anyone who has seen me has seen the Father”). The one who asks for directions to the prophet is already at the prophet’s feet; the one who seeks God has already been found by him.
19: Samuel’s “I am the seer” (ʾānōkî hārōʾeh) is the first of the prophet’s self-identifications that culminate in Christ’s “I am” statements. Samuel says: I am the one you were looking for; come with me; you will know everything. The structure of the divine self-disclosure — “I am what you are seeking; come and you will find more than you imagined” — is the deep pattern of every encounter with YHWH and Christ: “I am the way, the truth, and the life” (John 14:6); “I am the resurrection and the life” (John 11:25); “I am the bread of life” (John 6:35).
20: Samuel’s declaration — “for whom all Israel desires — is it not you and all your family?” — reveals the scale of what Saul is receiving: the desire of the entire people. The nation’s longing for a king who will deliver them is concentrated in Saul. The NT frames Christ as the fulfillment of the deepest desire of the nations: “the desire of all nations shall come” (Haggai 2:7 KJV) — the one in whom every longing for justice, peace, and divine presence is satisfied. Saul partially answers the surface desire; Christ answers the desire at its root.
21: Saul’s self-deprecation — “I am from Benjamin, the smallest tribe; my family is the least of all Benjamin’s clans” — is the pattern of the elect responding to divine calling with awareness of their inadequacy: Moses (“who am I?” Exodus 3:11), Gideon (“my clan is the weakest in Manasseh”, Judges 6:15), Jeremiah (“I do not know how to speak; I am too young”, Jeremiah 1:6). The theme of the elect being small/least is fulfilled in Christ’s Incarnation: the King of the universe coming as a vulnerable infant in Bethlehem of Judah — a small town, a carpenter’s family, an obscure province. Paul cites this pattern directly: “God chose the weak things of the world to shame the strong” (1 Corinthians 1:27).
22: Samuel places Saul at the head of the table among the invited guests — the last shall be first, the one who came looking for donkeys is seated at the place of honor. This is the table-reversal that runs through the entire biblical narrative and culminates in Christ’s parables (Luke 14:7-11: “when you are invited, take the lowest place... for whoever exalts himself will be humbled, and whoever humbles himself will be exalted”) and in the messianic banquet where the last invited are brought into the feast (Matthew 22:9-10). The unexpected guest given the place of honor is a structural type of grace.
23: The reserved portion — set aside by divine command before Saul arrived — is a shadow of the messianic preparation. Samuel tells the cook to bring “the portion I gave you — the one I told you to set aside.” YHWH had prepared the meal for the king-elect before his arrival, just as the kingdom was prepared for Christ “from the creation of the world” (Matthew 25:34). The meal that was held in anticipation of the right guest: “I confer on you a kingdom, just as my Father conferred one on me” (Luke 22:29).
24: A shadow of the messianic banquet: the reserved thigh-portion set aside specifically for Saul, presented at the appointed time. Samuel’s words — “it has been reserved for you... from the time I invited the guests” — articulate predestinated provision: before the feast began, the king’s portion was kept. The messianic meal in Isaiah 25:6 — “on this mountain the LORD Almighty will prepare a feast of rich food for all peoples” — is the full realization of what this reserved meal anticipates. Christ at the Last Supper explicitly frames the meal within anticipation of its fulfillment: “I will not eat it again until it finds fulfillment in the kingdom of God” (Luke 22:16).
25: Samuel and Saul talking on the rooftop — the private conversation between the prophet and the elect king before the public anointing. The intimate disclosure before the public commission is the pattern of prophetic preparation: YHWH speaks privately before acting publicly. Christ’s private preparation with the Father (the prayer in Gethsemane, John 17’s high-priestly prayer) precedes his public suffering and glorification. The rooftop conversation — above the common level, between the prophet and the king — is the space of divine-human exchange that the Incarnation makes permanent: “We have seen his glory” (John 1:14).
26: The early rising and sending-out continues the pattern of prophetic dispatch. Samuel sending Saul on his way after the private encounter is the model of discipleship commissioning: receive instruction in private, go out in public. The sequence of revelation → private preparation → public sending runs through the NT discipleship pattern (the disciples trained privately with Jesus, then sent out in Acts 1:8). The dawn-rising of the prophet to send the anointed one anticipates the dawn of resurrection: the early morning encounter between the women and the risen Christ (Mark 16:2; John 20:1), after which he sends them out.
27: The dismissal of the servant so that YHWH’s word may be given privately — “send the servant on ahead” — creates the space for prophetic disclosure to the elect. The private word of God to the chosen one, sealed apart from bystanders, is the pattern of prophetic commissioning: YHWH speaks to Moses apart from the camp, to Elijah alone at Horeb, to Isaiah in his throne-room vision. Christ similarly withdraws for private disclosure: the Transfiguration is shown to three disciples apart from the rest (Matthew 17:1); the resurrection appears first in private before the public confirmation. The private word that constitutes the messianic commission is foundational to the public messianic mission.
